describe what a tracker daw does differently than a modern daw, & what other types of daws & timeline music softwares are there?
A
Tracker is essentially a music sequencer that looks and acts like a spreadsheet, whereas a Modern DAW (Digital Audio Workstation) looks and acts like a multi-track tape recorder combined with a piano roll. 
While both output music, their core philosophy differs: Trackers are about "programming" notes numerically, while modern DAWs are about "arranging" audio visually. 
1. The Core Differences: Tracker vs. Modern DAW 
Feature Tracker (e.g., Renoise, OpenMPT)Modern DAW (e.g., Logic, Ableton, Pro Tools)
Visual FlowVertical. The timeline scrolls from top to bottom.Horizontal. The timeline moves from left to right.
Note EntryQWERTY Keyboard. You type notes and commands rapidly using computer keys (e.g., C-4 01 A0F).Mouse & MIDI. You draw notes on a "Piano Roll" or record them live via a MIDI keyboard.
Data ViewHexadecimal Columns. All data (note, instrument, volume, effects) is visible simultaneously in precise columns.Abstractions. Notes are blocks; volume and effects are often hidden in automation lanes or mixer windows.
ModulationPer-Step. You can drastically change a sound on every single 16th note (e.g., pan left, pitch shift up, reverse) by typing a code next to the note.Curves. You typically draw smooth automation curves over time to change parameters.
WorkflowStep-Sequencing. precise, mathematical, and extremely fast for complex, rapid-fire drums (Jungle, Breakcore).Linear Recording. Intuitive for recording bands, vocals, and "playing" instruments naturally.
The "Tracker" Vibe:
Imagine composing music in Excel. You don't "drag" a clip to move it; you copy and paste a block of text. Because you are typing commands, expert tracker users can input complex drum patterns faster than they can think them. This is why genres like Jungle, Drum & Bass, and IDM (Aphex Twin, Venetian Snares) are historically rooted in trackers—they allow for intricate per-step manipulation that would be tedious to draw by hand in a modern DAW. 
2. Other Types of Music Software & Timelines 
Beyond the binary of "Tracker vs. DAW," music software falls into several distinct evolutionary branches. 
A. Linear DAWs (The "Tape Recorder" Paradigm) 
The standard for professional studio recording. They prioritize a linear timeline that mimics analog tape, making them best for recording live bands, mixing, and film scoring. 
  • Examples: Pro Tools, Logic Pro, Cubase, Reaper, Studio One. 
B. Non-Linear / Clip-Based DAWs 
These blur the line between studio tool and instrument. Instead of just a timeline, they feature a "Session" or "Clip" view where you can trigger loops out of order. This makes them the industry standard for electronic music production and live DJ performances. 
  • Examples: Ableton Live, Bitwig Studio. 
C. The "Groovebox" / Pattern Sequencers 
Originating from drum machines rather than tape recorders, these focus on building short loops (patterns) and chaining them together. 
  • Examples: FL Studio (formerly FruityLoops, famous for its step-sequencer workflow), Maschine, MPC Software. 
D. Modular / Rack Environments 
These simulate a physical rack of hardware synthesizers. You manually "wire" cables between devices to create sound. The timeline is often secondary to the sound design environment. 
  • Examples: Reason (famous for its tab-to-flip rack interface), VCV Rack, Cherry Audio Voltage Modular. 
E. Audio Programming Environments 
For when "software" isn't enough and you want to build the instrument itself. These use code or visual "nodes" to generate audio mathematically. This is heavily used in academic computer music and by experimental artists (like Radiohead’s Jonny Greenwood). 
  • Visual (Dataflow): Max/MSP, Pure Data (Open Source).
  • Code-Based (Live Coding): SuperCollider, Sonic Pi, TidalCycles. 
F. Score Writers (Notation Software) 
These use a traditional sheet music staff rather than a piano roll or timeline. They are "Word Processors" for music, used by classical composers and orchestrators. 
  • Examples: Dorico, Sibelius, Finale, MuseScore. 
G. Audio Editors (Destructive Editing) 
Unlike DAWs, which are "non-destructive" (changing a clip doesn't delete the original file), these permanently alter the audio file. They are used for cleaning up dialogue, mastering, or scientific analysis. 
  • Examples: Adobe Audition, Audacity, Sound Forge. 
Are you looking to try a specific workflow, or are you trying to identify a piece of software you've seen used?

Ableton also does something similar as one of it's main functions
You’re likely thinking of Ableton’s
Session View. 
While modern DAWs usually use a horizontal "Arrangement View," Ableton’s Session View is a vertical, grid-based interface that shares a similar philosophy with trackers—treating music as a collection of modular blocks rather than one long tape recording. 
How Session View Bridges the Gap 
  • Vertical Orientation: Like a tracker, Session View flows vertically. Each column is a track, and each "cell" (called a Clip) contains a musical idea—a drum loop, a bassline, or a melody.
  • Pattern-Based Thinking: Trackers use "patterns" that you chain together. In Ableton, you use Scenes (horizontal rows). When you trigger a Scene, every clip in that row starts playing simultaneously, allowing you to build song structures out of reusable blocks.
  • Live Manipulation: Trackers are famous for "command effects" (like instant retriggers or pitch slides). Ableton replicates this through Follow Actions, which allow clips to trigger other clips automatically or randomly, creating the same kind of generative, complex behavior found in tracker-based genres like Jungle. 
Is it a "Tracker"? 
Technically, no. Even in Session View, Ableton still uses a Piano Roll and Automation Curves to edit the data inside those clips. A true tracker replaces the piano roll entirely with a list of hexadecimal numbers and text codes. 
However, if you love the tracker workflow but want Ableton's power, there are Max for Live devices that actually embed a functional tracker directly into an Ableton track, giving you the best of both worlds. 
Would you like to see some examples of these "Tracker-style" plugins for Ableton, or are you more interested in how to use the Session View for live performance?
